slides.title: One Name, Many Behaviors
slides.body: **Polymorphism** means different objects can share the same method name, but each responds in its own way. Think of a TV remote — same volume button, different brand, different circuitry underneath.
slides.tip: poly = many, morph = form — many forms from one interface

slides.title: Same Method, Different Result
slides.body: Give `Dog`, `Cat`, and `Duck` a `speak()` method each. Call `speak()` on any of them and you get the *right* sound — no extra logic needed.
slides.tip: Same method name, totally different behavior — that's the whole idea

slides.title: Loop Once, Handle Everything
slides.body: Put different objects in a **list** and call the same method in a loop. Python figures out which version to run for each object — you write the loop once.
slides.tip: One loop handles any number of types — even ones you add later

slides.title: Duck Typing: Behavior Over Type
slides.body: Python uses **duck typing**: if an object has the method you need, Python calls it — no ID check required. A `Robot` with `speak()` fits right in alongside the animals.
slides.tip: "If it quacks like a duck, it's a duck" — Python only cares about the method, not the class

slides.title: You Already Use Polymorphism!
slides.body: `len()` and `+` work on strings, lists, and numbers — same call, different behavior per type. You've been writing polymorphic code all along!
slides.tip: Polymorphism isn't exotic — it's everywhere in everyday Python
